questions.question: Why do physicists divide physics into branches?
options: Because physics is so big it's easier to focus on specific areas
Because different branches use different math
Because some branches are harder than others
Because different branches study different planets
questions.explanation: Physics covers too much to study all at once. Branches let physicists specialize and go deep in one area.

questions.question: Your car moves forward when you press the gas. Which branch explains this?
questions.explanation: Classical mechanics explains motion and forces. A car accelerating is all about forces and motion.

questions.question: Electricity and Magnetism is the same branch as Waves and Optics.
questions.explanation: No, they're different branches. Electricity and Magnetism focuses on charged particles and fields. Waves and Optics focuses on light and sound.

questions.question: Where does Quantum Mechanics matter most?
options: When studying atoms and tiny particles
questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics is about things at the atomic scale. Atoms, electrons, particles—that's where quantum rules apply.

questions.question: All branches of physics are equally important for your everyday life.
questions.explanation: Yes! Your phone (electricity), your car (classical mechanics), your stove (thermodynamics), light and sound (waves), even the atoms in your body (quantum). You interact with all branches every day.
